narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 49% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. You have a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.3, which is above the typical student's 3.21, along with high family income, full-time enrollment plans, and your background as a white female—traits that align closely with or better than many applicants. However, your LSAT score of 22.5 is notably lower than the average of 36.7, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both sit at the bottom (1st decile versus averages around 5.5-5.7), placing you well below typical student performance in those early academic rankings. When the model evaluated your profile, it identified your LSAT score as the single most influential factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it, your low Year 3 decile had the next biggest impact in the same direction, followed very closely by your low Year 1 decile, both signaling to the model potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Your family income level ranked fourth in influence, also nudging toward a failure prediction despite being high, with your undergraduate GPA fifth—surprisingly contributing slightly against you even though it's solid compared to peers. Your racial background came next in sway, still tilting toward failure, while your full-time status had minimal pull, and your gender as female provided the one small counterbalance, gently favoring a passing prediction. Overall, these top factors—especially the low LSAT and early-year deciles—outweighed your strengths, making your risk profile higher than most students who get approved. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on what you can control: intensive test preparation to address patterns seen in your low LSAT and deciles, perhaps through practice exams or tutoring, since those reflect skills vital for the bar. Building better study habits now could mimic higher decile performance, and seeking bar-specific prep courses tailored for students with your GPA strength might help bridge the gap. With targeted effort in these areas, you could shift your profile toward the successful majority and reapply stronger next time—we're rooting for you.
